questions.text: The value of impedance in a series LCR circuit at resonance is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Impedance in a Series LCR Circuit:Impedance (Z) in a series LCR circuit represents the total opposition to the flow of alternating current (AC) and is calculated as:Z = √(R² + (XL - XC)²)where:R: ResistanceXL: Inductive reactanceXC: Capacitive reactanceResonance in a Series LCR Circuit:Resonance occurs at a specific frequency (f_r) where the inductive reactance (XL) exactly cancels out the capacitive reactance (XC). Mathematically, this means:XL = XCDuring resonance, the imaginary component of the impedance equation (XL - XC) becomes zero. As a result, the square root operation in the impedance formula simplifies to:Z = √(R²)Therefore, at resonance, the impedance of the series LCR circuit reduces to:Z = RConclusion:Since resistance (R) is a positive value, the impedance of the series LCR circuit at resonance becomes minimum and equal to the resistance (R) of the circuit
Impedance is minimized, not maximized, at resonance
This would occur only in open circuits (infinite resistance), not during resonance
Impedance becomes equal to the resistance (R), which is not zero

questions.explanation: Impedance in a Series LCR Circuit:Impedance (Z) in a series LCR circuit represents the total opposition to the flow of alternating current (AC) and is calculated as:Z = √(R² + (XL - XC)²)where:R: ResistanceXL: Inductive reactanceXC: Capacitive reactanceResonance in a Series LCR Circuit:Resonance occurs at a specific frequency (f_r) where the inductive reactance (XL) exactly cancels out the capacitive reactance (XC). Mathematically, this means:XL = XCDuring resonance, the imaginary component of the impedance equation (XL - XC) becomes zero. As a result, the square root operation in the impedance formula simplifies to:Z = √(R²)Therefore, at resonance, the impedance of the series LCR circuit reduces to:Z = RConclusion:Since resistance (R) is a positive value, the impedance of the series LCR circuit at resonance becomes minimum and equal to the resistance (R) of the circuit

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The charge on the antenna changes as the potential alternates.

As the charging potential alternates, the charge on the antenna also alternates in polarity. Therefore, the charge constantly reverses its polarity.

options.explanation: Components of Impedance:The impedance (Z) in an R-L circuit is the total opposition to the flow of AC current and is calculated as:Z = √(R² + XL²)where:R: Resistance (Ω)XL: Inductive Reactance (Ω)Impact of Frequency on Inductive Reactance:Inductive reactance (XL) is directly proportional to the frequency (f) of the AC source:XL = 2πfLTherefore, as the frequency (f) increases, the inductive reactance (XL) also increases.Effect on Impedance:Since XL is squared in the impedance formula, its increase due to rising frequency has a greater impact on the overall impedance (Z) compared to the constant resistance (R). This leads to an increase in the total impedance of the circuit.Conclusion:With a higher frequency, the inductive reactance dominates the opposition, causing the total impedance of the R-L circuit to increase

As explained, the increase in XL leads to a rise in impedance, not a decrease

options.explanation: Capacitive Reactance:In an AC circuit, a capacitor offers opposition to current flow through the property of capacitive reactance (XC), measured in ohms (Ω). questions.text: Power in an AC circuit is given by
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This expression does not account for the power factor and the phase difference between voltage and current

In an AC circuit, the real power (P) is given by the product of voltage (V), current (I), and the power factor (cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current, θ). Therefore, the correct expression for power in an AC circuit is P=VIcos(θ).
This expression does not account for the power factor and the phase difference between voltage and current
The tangent function is not used to calculate power in an AC circuit

questions.explanation: In an AC circuit, the real power (P) is given by the product of voltage (V), current (I), and the power factor (cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current, θ). Therefore, the correct expression for power in an AC circuit is P=VIcos(θ).

questions.text: In a three-phase AC supply, the P.D across two live wires is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value does not represent the relationship between phase voltage and line voltage in a three-phase system
This value is not a standard relationship in three-phase systems

In a three-phase AC supply, the phase voltage (V) is the voltage between any one of the live wires and the neutral wire. The line voltage (P.D across two live wires) is √3 times the phase voltage. Therefore, the correct option is √3V
This value is not relevant to the relationship between phase voltage and line voltage in a three-phase system.

questions.explanation: In a three-phase AC supply, the phase voltage (V) is the voltage between any one of the live wires and the neutral wire. The line voltage (P.D across two live wires) is √3 times the phase voltage. Therefore, the correct option is √3V

questions.text: In alternating current the average value of current in cycle is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an alternating current (AC) circuit, the flow of electric current periodically changes direction. The current oscillates back and forth, completing one full cycle when it returns to its original direction. The average value of the current over one complete cycle is zero. The average value of the current in an AC circuit over one complete cycle is zero due to the continuous oscillation and symmetrical nature of the alternating current waveform. The average current is given by the formula:Iavg= 1/T∫0Ti(t)dtIavg is the average current,T is the total time period,i(t) is the instantaneous current at time tThe integral represents the area under the current vs. time curve over one complete cycle.
Due to the reason stated above, this option is incorrect.
Due to the reason stated above, this option is incorrect.
Due to the reason stated above, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In an alternating current (AC) circuit, the flow of electric current periodically changes direction. The current oscillates back and forth, completing one full cycle when it returns to its original direction. The average value of the current over one complete cycle is zero. The average value of the current in an AC circuit over one complete cycle is zero due to the continuous oscillation and symmetrical nature of the alternating current waveform. The average current is given by the formula:Iavg= 1/T∫0Ti(t)dtIavg is the average current,T is the total time period,i(t) is the instantaneous current at time tThe integral represents the area under the current vs. time curve over one complete cycle.

questions.text: In L.C.R series A.C circuit, the phase angle between current and voltage is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is a general statement that includes all possible phase angles, not specific to the LCR circuit. The phase angle in an LCR circuit can vary based on the values of L, C, and R.
This is not necessarily true for all LCR circuits. The phase angle can be greater or less than π/2, depending on the circuit parameters.
This is not necessarily true. The phase angle depends on the reactance and resistance in the circuit.

In an LCR (inductor, capacitor, resistor) series AC circuit, the phase angle between current and voltage depends on the values of inductance, capacitance, and resistance in the circuit. The phase angle is given by the arc tangent of the ratio of the reactance difference to the resistance:Tan (ϕ)= XL-XC/RWhere:XL is the inductive resistance.XC is the conductive resistance.R is the resistance.

questions.explanation: In an LCR (inductor, capacitor, resistor) series AC circuit, the phase angle between current and voltage depends on the values of inductance, capacitance, and resistance in the circuit. The phase angle is given by the arc tangent of the ratio of the reactance difference to the resistance:Tan (ϕ)= XL-XC/RWhere:XL is the inductive resistance.XC is the conductive resistance.R is the resistance.

questions.text: in RLC series circuit when the frequency of AC source is very low the circuit is a/an
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an RLC series circuit, when the frequency of the AC source is very low, the circuit behaves like a capacitor-dominated circuit, also known as a capacitive circuit.At low frequencies, the capacitive reactance (Xc) is high, and the inductive reactance (Xl) is low. Since Xc is high, it impedes the flow of current, causing the circuit to behave primarily as a capacitive circuit. The total impedance (Z) of the circuit is close to the capacitive reactance.The formula for capacitive reactance is:Xc = 1 / (2 * π * f * C)where f is the frequency and C is the capacitance. As the frequency decreases, the capacitive reactance increases, making it the dominant factor in the circuit

questions.explanation: In an RLC series circuit, when the frequency of the AC source is very low, the circuit behaves like a capacitor-dominated circuit, also known as a capacitive circuit.At low frequencies, the capacitive reactance (Xc) is high, and the inductive reactance (Xl) is low. Since Xc is high, it impedes the flow of current, causing the circuit to behave primarily as a capacitive circuit. The total impedance (Z) of the circuit is close to the capacitive reactance.The formula for capacitive reactance is:Xc = 1 / (2 * π * f * C)where f is the frequency and C is the capacitance. As the frequency decreases, the capacitive reactance increases, making it the dominant factor in the circuit

questions.text: in AC through capacitor circuit the v and q are as
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an AC circuit with a capacitor, the relationship between voltage (V) and charge (Q) is as follows:a. Voltage (V) leads charge (Q) by 90 degrees.This is because in a capacitor, the current (I) leads the voltage (V) by 90 degrees, and the current (I) is directly proportional to the rate of change of charge (Q). Mathematically, this can be represented as:I = C * (dV/dt)Where C is the capacitance, dV/dt is the rate of change of voltage with respect to time, and I is the current.Since current (I) is in phase with the rate of change of charge (dQ/dt), it means that the charge (Q) lags the current (I) by 90 degrees. As a result, voltage (V) leads charge (Q) by 90 degrees

options.text: V and q are in phase

questions.text: at resonance, the phase angle for RLC series resonance circuit equals
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an RLC series resonance circuit, at resonance, the phase angle between the voltage and current equals:a. 0 degrees (in-phase)Resonance in an RLC series circuit occurs when the inductive reactance (Xl) and capacitive reactance (Xc) are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. This happens at the resonant frequency (fr), which can be calculated as:fr = 1 / (2 * π * √(LC))Where L is the inductance and C is the capacitance.At resonance, the net reactance (Xl - Xc) becomes zero, and the total impedance (Z) of the circuit is equal to the resistance (R) of the resistor. Since the impedance is purely resistive, the voltage (V) and current (I) are in phase with each other, resulting in a phase angle of 0 degrees. This means that the voltage and current waveforms reach their maximum and minimum values at the same time

This happens at the resonant frequency (fr), which can be calculated as:fr = 1 / (2 * π * √(LC))Where L is the inductance and C is the capacitance.At resonance, the net reactance (Xl - Xc) becomes zero, and the total impedance (Z) of the circuit is equal to the resistance (R) of the resistor. Since the impedance is purely resistive, the voltage (V) and current (I) are in phase with each other, resulting in a phase angle of 0 degrees. This means that the voltage and current waveforms reach their maximum and minimum values at the same time

questions.text: The inductive reactance of the coil having inductance of 0.5 henry in which AC of 50 Hz flows is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The inductive reactance of a coil is given by \(X_L = 2πfL\), where \(f\) is the frequency of the AC and \(L\) is the inductance of the coil. Substituting the given values, \(X_L = 2π(50)(0.5) ≈ 157\, \Omega\).
The calculation for inductive reactance is incorrect. The correct value is \(X_L ≈ 157\, \Omega\), not 1.57 ohm.

questions.text: In an RLC series circuit, when the frequency of the AC source is very low, the circuit is a/an
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When the frequency of the AC source is very low, the inductive reactance (\(X_L\)) and capacitive reactance (\(X_C\)) in the circuit become negligible compared to the resistance (\(R\)). In this case, the circuit behaves almost like a pure resistive circuit.

When the frequency of the AC source is very low, the inductive reactance (\(X_L\)) becomes negligible compared to the resistance (\(R\)), but the capacitive reactance (\(X_C\)) may still have an effect on the circuit. Therefore, the circuit is not purely capacitive.

When the frequency of the AC source is very low, the capacitive reactance (\(X_C\)) becomes negligible compared to the resistance (\(R\)), but the inductive reactance (\(X_L\)) may still have an effect on the circuit. Therefore, the circuit is not purely inductive.

A resonant circuit is one in which the inductive reactance (\(X_L\)) is equal to the capacitive reactance (\(X_C\)), resulting in resonance. This condition does not apply when the frequency of the AC source is very low.

questions.explanation: When the frequency of the AC source is very low, the inductive reactance (\(X_L\)) and capacitive reactance (\(X_C\)) in the circuit become negligible compared to the resistance (\(R\)). In this case, the circuit behaves almost like a pure resistive circuit.

questions.text: Which of the following makes the motion of a perpetual motion machine a physical impossibility
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The first law of thermodynamics, also known as the law of conservation of energy, states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed from one form to another. While perpetual motion machines violate the first law by producing energy indefinitely without an external source, this law alone does not make perpetual motion machines physically impossible.

The second law of thermodynamics states that in any energy transfer or transformation, there will always be an increase in the entropy of the universe. Perpetual motion machines violate this law because they aim to produce energy indefinitely without any input, which would decrease entropy over time, contradicting the second law.

The third law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero temperature is zero. While this law provides insight into the behavior of systems at absolute zero, it does not directly relate to the impossibility of perpetual motion machines.

Both the first and second laws of thermodynamics contribute to the understanding of why perpetual motion machines are impossible. While the first law addresses the conservation of energy, the second law specifically addresses the increase in entropy and the impossibility of achieving perpetual motion.

questions.explanation: The second law of thermodynamics states that in any energy transfer or transformation, there will always be an increase in the entropy of the universe. Perpetual motion machines violate this law because they aim to produce energy indefinitely without any input, which would decrease entropy over time, contradicting the second law.

questions.text: The process of combining low-frequency signal with high-frequency carrier waves is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Rectification is the process of converting alternating current (AC) to direct current (DC), typically by using a diode or a rectifier circuit.
Amplification is the process of increasing the magnitude of a signal, typically done using electronic amplifiers.

Modulation is the process of varying the characteristics of a carrier wave (high-frequency signal) in proportion to the information signal (low-frequency signal) being transmitted. Common types of modulation include amplitude modulation (AM), frequency modulation (FM), and phase modulation (PM).
Magnification is not a term commonly used in the context of combining signals. It typically refers to the process of enlarging or making something appear larger.

questions.explanation: Modulation is the process of varying the characteristics of a carrier wave (high-frequency signal) in proportion to the information signal (low-frequency signal) being transmitted. Common types of modulation include amplitude modulation (AM), frequency modulation (FM), and phase modulation (PM).
